meta.name: Akatsuki PhD Task Bank – Shadow Research Missions
meta.description: Core PhD missions for research question development, literature review, theory, methodology, data collection, analysis, writing, revision, advisor communication, and academic identity.

tasks.title: Scan the Shadows for One Research Gap
tasks.subject: Research Question Development
tasks.successCriteria: One clearly articulated gap statement written in your notes.
tasks.failureCondition: Browsing articles without capturing a specific gap.
tasks.reward: Progress toward a sharper research question.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write a 3-sentence gap statement from any one article.

steps.label: Open your main topic notes or concept map.
Search for 3–5 recent articles on your topic.
Skim abstracts and note what they DO cover.
Write 3 bullets on what seems missing or underdeveloped.
Draft one gap statement starting with “What is not yet clear is…”

tasks.notes: Use this when you feel your question is fuzzy or too broad.

tasks.title: Refine One Overgrown Research Question
tasks.subject: Research Question Development
tasks.successCriteria: One research question rewritten to be specific, feasible, and aligned with your method.
tasks.failureCondition: Keeping a vague or multi-part question unchanged.
tasks.reward: Sharper direction and easier methodology decisions.
tasks.recoveryMission: Rewrite the question into two shorter candidate versions.

steps.label: Copy your current research question into a fresh note.
Underline vague terms (e.g., ‘impact’, ‘effect’, ‘people’).
Specify population, context, and key construct.
Check if the question can be answered with your planned method.
Write the refined version and label it v2.

tasks.notes: Use when you feel your question is doing too much at once.

tasks.title: Hunt 5 Relevant Articles for One Thread
tasks.subject: Literature Review
tasks.successCriteria: 5 relevant articles saved with full citation details.
tasks.failureCondition: Collecting fewer than 3 usable sources or not saving citations.
tasks.reward: Expanded source bank for one conceptual thread.
tasks.recoveryMission: Find 2 anchor papers only and save them properly.

steps.label: Open your library database or Google Scholar.
Search one focused keyword cluster for your thread.
Save top 10 promising results.
Screen abstracts and discard weak fits.
Keep the best 5 and save PDFs + citations.

tasks.notes: Pair this with a reading mission to avoid hoarding PDFs.

tasks.title: Extract Key Insights from One Article
tasks.subject: Literature Review
tasks.successCriteria: One article summarized in 5–8 sentences with 3 key takeaways.
tasks.reward: One more article fully integrated into your mental map.

steps.label: Open the article and read abstract + conclusion.
Scan methods and findings sections.
Highlight 3–5 key arguments or results.
Write a short synthesis paragraph in your notes.
Tag the note with topic, method, and relevance.

tasks.notes: Use the same note structure for every article to keep things consistent.

tasks.title: Update the Literature Matrix for One Concept
tasks.subject: Literature Review
tasks.successCriteria: Matrix updated with at least 3 new rows or cleaned entries.
tasks.failureCondition: Leaving new articles unintegrated into your matrix.
tasks.reward: Clearer overview of who said what and where.
tasks.recoveryMission: Add at least 1 new article to the matrix.

steps.label: Open your literature matrix (sheet or table).
Pick one concept or thread to focus on.
Add rows for any newly read articles.
Fill in columns: theory, method, sample, key findings.
Highlight gaps or contradictions you notice.

tasks.notes: This mission keeps your review from becoming a pile of disconnected notes.

tasks.title: Map Mechanisms for One Core Concept
tasks.subject: Theory Building
tasks.successCriteria: A visual or written map of how one concept operates in your context.
tasks.failureCondition: Keeping the concept as a vague label without mechanisms.
tasks.reward: Stronger theoretical backbone for your argument.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write a 5-bullet list of possible mechanisms.

steps.label: Choose one focal concept (e.g., identity threat, AI displacement).
List all factors that might influence it.
Sketch arrows or relationships between factors and outcomes.
Note any rival explanations or alternative paths.
Write a short narrative explaining the mechanism map.

tasks.notes: Use this before writing theory sections or propositions.

tasks.title: Compare Two Possible Research Designs
tasks.subject: Methodology
tasks.successCriteria: A short comparison note with pros/cons and a tentative choice.
tasks.failureCondition: Staying stuck between designs without writing down tradeoffs.
tasks.reward: Clarity on which design better fits your question.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write a 5-bullet pros/cons list for each design.

steps.label: Name the two designs you’re considering.
List strengths and weaknesses for each.
Check alignment with your research question and data access.
Write a short paragraph recommending one design.
Save this note for advisor discussion.

tasks.notes: Use this before committing to your final method section.

tasks.title: Run One Recruitment Push
tasks.subject: Data Collection
tasks.successCriteria: At least one concrete recruitment action completed (emails sent, posts published, or invitations delivered).
tasks.failureCondition: Planning recruitment without actually sending anything.
tasks.recoveryMission: Send at least 2 invitations or one reminder message.

steps.label: Review your target participant list or channels.
Draft or refine your recruitment message.
Send emails, messages, or post in agreed channels.
Log who you contacted and when.
Schedule a follow-up reminder if needed.

tasks.notes: Keep a simple spreadsheet of recruitment attempts and responses.

tasks.title: Code One Transcript Batch
tasks.subject: Data Analysis
tasks.successCriteria: One transcript batch coded with a short memo.
tasks.recoveryMission: Code just 1–2 pages and write a 3-bullet memo.

steps.label: Open the transcript batch and your codebook.
Code the first section carefully, checking definitions.
Continue coding through the batch, marking uncertainties.
Write a memo on emerging patterns or surprises.
Save coded file + memo in your analysis folder.

tasks.notes: Use consistent memo headings: patterns, tensions, questions.

tasks.title: Draft 500 Words of Literature Review
tasks.subject: Academic Writing
tasks.successCriteria: At least 500 usable words added to the literature review.
tasks.failureCondition: Only editing existing text without adding new content.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write 150 words based on one subsection outline.

steps.label: Open your literature review outline.
Choose one subsection to focus on.
Gather relevant article notes for that subsection.
Write a topic sentence and 2–3 evidence paragraphs.
Add a synthesis sentence and transition.
Save version and note word count.

tasks.notes: Use a timer and protect this block from interruptions.

tasks.title: Run One Focused Revision Pass
tasks.subject: Revision & Submission
tasks.successCriteria: One section revised with feedback addressed.
tasks.reward: Cleaner draft and reduced revision backlog.

steps.label: Open the draft and feedback document.
Choose one section or cluster of comments.
Address comments one by one, updating the text.
Mark resolved comments as done.
Write a short note summarizing what changed.

tasks.notes: Use this mission repeatedly instead of trying to revise everything at once.

tasks.title: Prepare Advisor Update Scroll
tasks.subject: Advisor Communication
tasks.successCriteria: Short update with wins, blockers, and questions ready.
tasks.reward: Clearer advisor conversations and better guidance.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write a 3-bullet email draft summarizing progress.

steps.label: List progress since last advisor contact.
List current blockers or decisions needed.
Write 2–3 specific questions you want answered.
Attach any relevant draft or notes.
Save this as your meeting agenda or email draft.

tasks.notes: Use the same structure every time to make meetings efficient.

tasks.title: Refine Your Academic Bio
tasks.subject: Academic Identity Building
tasks.successCriteria: One updated bio version saved (short or long).
tasks.failureCondition: Using outdated or vague bios for talks or profiles.
tasks.reward: Stronger sense of your scholarly identity.
tasks.recoveryMission: Write a 3-sentence version of your current research focus.

steps.label: Open your current bio (short or long).
Highlight outdated phrases or missing elements.
Update research focus, methods, and key interests.
Save a short (2–3 sentence) and longer (1 paragraph) version.
Store them in a quick-access folder.

tasks.notes: Use this before conferences, applications, or new profiles.
